React Basics: useState, the One Hook You Absolutely Have to Get (7)
State is one of the most important things in React Hooks. How important? In short, the moment you need to change something that shows up on screen, you can't escape React state.
Before I get into React state, I want to jog the memory of any Vue developers reading this. In Vue, when we need to bind data to a field on screen, we reach for
to keep the data in sync.

directive you can update the data and trigger a re-render to refresh the screen, all in one go. That's what we call two-way binding. If you're curious about how that binding actually works under the hood, I've written about it before:
A Gentle Intro to Vue 2 Two-Way Binding — Concept and Implementation
A Gentle Intro to Vue 3 Two-Way Binding — Concept and Implementation
One thing to note: the underlying implementation differs between Vue 2 and Vue 3 — Vue 2 uses
. So they're not the same thing.
So what about React? Is it similar? Let's look at how React deals with data.
We've covered a lot of React stuff in the earlier chapters, and I touched on Vue's two-way binding too. But honestly, we haven't actually started on React Hooks yet — not a single hook so far.
So now it's time to introduce the first one: the State Hook.
"State" basically means the status of something — specifically, the status of your data. In that sense it's a lot like Vue's
, because we pass in an initial value for the state to watch.
There's a small difference in React though. The State Hook is really just referring to the
function. The basic syntax looks like this:

. The first one is the plain data used for rendering on screen, and the second one is how you tell React the data has changed, so it should re-render.
For example, say you've got a button on screen and clicking it should swap the content. If you don't use

The reason is that if you want to change the value, you have to go through the second destructured variable — that's the correct way to do it. And one thing to watch out for: that second variable is a function.
See the Pen (64) React State Hook by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
What this shows is that in React, you have to
decide when to trigger the function yourself
— that's the only way the screen re-renders.

) exists specifically to trigger that re-render, and this is a different situation from Vue. In Vue, changing the data updates the screen immediately. In React, you get to decide when the screen updates. This is exactly why people say React's data is one-way bound: we just bind the data to the screen, and even if the data updates, we can choose not to update the view. As long as you don't call that specific function, the screen won't change. Vue is the opposite — any time the data changes, the screen will update (unless you wrote something wrong or stepped on a landmine).
Before you start writing hooks, remember the React Hook rules I mentioned in earlier chapters:
Only call them at the top level of a function
Only use them in Function Components

Sharp-eyed as you are, you've probably noticed arrays and objects aren't in there. That's because those two need to be written a slightly different way:

Array Value: { arr }

Array Value: { arr }

Object Value: { JSON.stringify(obj, null, 2) }
After all these examples and bits of practice, you can see why people always say React leans hard on your JavaScript fundamentals. Just
alone forces you to understand array destructuring — never mind object destructuring and all the rest.

State is one of the most important things in React Hooks. How important? In short, the moment you need to change something that shows up on screen, you can't escape React state. In this post I'll walk through useState — starting from Vue's v-model so the Vue folks have something familiar to anchor to — then dig into how React handles data, why it's called one-way binding, and what types you can throw at useState.
